title: How Do I Love Thee? (Sonnet 43)
author: Elizabeth Barrett Browning
lines: How do I love thee? Let me count the ways.
I love thee to the depth and breadth and height
My soul can reach, when feeling out of sight
For the ends of being and ideal grace.
I love thee to the level of every day's
Most quiet need, by sun and candle-light.
I love thee freely, as men strive for right;
I love thee purely, as they turn from praise.
I love thee with the passion put to use
In my old griefs, and with my childhood's faith.
I love thee with a love I seemed to lose
With my lost saints. I love thee with the breath,
Smiles, tears, of all my life; and, if God choose,
I shall but love thee better after death.
background: Written by English poet Elizabeth Barrett Browning (1806–1861) as part of her 'Sonnets from the Portuguese' collection, composed during her secret courtship with poet Robert Browning. The collection was a deeply personal expression of her love for Robert. This sonnet, the 43rd, is among the most quoted love poems in the English language, celebrated for its tender inventory of love's boundless depth.

title: She Walks in Beauty
author: Lord Byron
lines: She walks in beauty, like the night
Of cloudless climes and starry skies;
And all that's best of dark and bright
Meet in her aspect and her eyes;
Thus mellowed to that tender light
Which heaven to gaudy day denies.
One shade the more, one ray the less,
Had half impaired the nameless grace
Which waves in every raven tress,
Or softly lightens o'er her face;
Where thoughts serenely sweet express,
How pure, how dear their dwelling-place.
And on that cheek, and o'er that brow,
So soft, so calm, yet eloquent,
The smiles that win, the tints that glow,
But tell of days in goodness spent,
A mind at peace with all below,
A heart whose love is innocent!
background: Written by George Gordon Byron, Lord Byron (1788–1824), one of the most celebrated Romantic poets. This poem was composed in 1814 after Byron saw his cousin by marriage, Mrs Robert John Wilmot, at a party wearing a black dress adorned with spangles. Struck by her beauty, he wrote this lyric the following morning. It remains one of the most admired tributes to feminine beauty ever written.

title: I Carry Your Heart With Me
author: E. E. Cummings
lines: i carry your heart with me (i carry it in
my heart) i am never without it (anywhere
i go you go, my dear; and whatever is done
by only me is your doing, my darling)
i fear no fate (for you are my fate, my sweet) i want
no world (for beautiful you are my world, my true)
and it's you are whatever a moon has always meant
and whatever a sun will always sing is you
here is the deepest secret nobody knows
(here is the root of the root and the bud of the bud
and the sky of the sky of a tree called life; which grows
higher than soul can hope or mind can hide)
and this is the wonder that's keeping the stars apart
i carry your heart (i carry it in my heart)
background: E. E. Cummings (1894–1962) was an American poet known for his experimental style, unconventional punctuation and lowercase typography. This poem, published in 1952, is considered his most romantic work. It expresses the idea that loving someone makes them an inseparable part of you. Its lyrical simplicity and emotional depth have made it a popular choice for weddings and declarations of love worldwide.

title: Sonnet 18
author: William Shakespeare
lines: Shall I compare thee to a summer's day?
Thou art more lovely and more temperate:
Rough winds do shake the darling buds of May,
And summer's lease hath all too short a date:
Sometime too hot the eye of heaven shines,
And often is his gold complexion dimm'd;
And every fair from fair sometime declines,
By chance, or nature's changing course untrimm'd;
But thy eternal summer shall not fade,
Nor lose possession of that fair thou ow'st,
Nor shall death brag thou wander'st in his shade,
When in eternal lines to time thou grow'st:
So long as men can breathe, or eyes can see,
So long lives this, and this gives life to thee.
background: William Shakespeare (1564–1616) is widely regarded as the greatest writer in the English language. Sonnet 18 is the most famous of his 154 sonnets, likely written in the 1590s. In it, Shakespeare argues that his beloved surpasses even the beauty of a summer's day, and that through the power of his poetry, their beauty will be immortalised forever — a radical and romantic idea for its time.

title: Tonight I Can Write
author: Pablo Neruda
lines: Tonight I can write the saddest lines.
Write, for example: 'The night is starry,
and the stars are blue, and shiver in the distance.'
The night wind revolves in the sky and sings.
Tonight I can write the saddest lines.
I loved her, and sometimes she loved me too.
Through nights like this one I held her in my arms.
I kissed her again and again under the endless sky.
She loved me, sometimes I loved her too.
How could one not have loved her great still eyes.
Tonight I can write the saddest lines.
To think that I do not have her. To feel that I have lost her.
To hear the immense night, still more immense without her.
And the verse falls to the soul like dew to the pasture.
background: Pablo Neruda (1904–1973) was a Chilean poet and Nobel Prize laureate, considered one of the greatest love poets of the 20th century. This poem is from his collection 'Twenty Love Poems and a Song of Despair' (1924), written when Neruda was just nineteen. It captures the ache of a love that has ended — the tension between memory and loss — and remains one of the most emotionally resonant love poems ever written.

title: A Red, Red Rose
author: Robert Burns
lines: O my Luve is like a red, red rose
That's newly sprung in June;
O my Luve is like the melody
That's sweetly played in tune.
So fair art thou, my bonnie lass,
So deep in luve am I;
And I will luve thee still, my dear,
Till a' the seas gang dry.
Till a' the seas gang dry, my dear,
And the rocks melt wi' the sun;
I will love thee still, my dear,
While the sands o' life shall run.
And fare thee weel, my only luve!
And fare thee weel awhile!
And I will come again, my luve,
Though it were ten thousand mile.
background: Robert Burns (1759–1796), Scotland's national poet, composed this beloved lyric around 1794, drawing on Scottish folk songs. Written in Scots dialect, it is a passionate declaration of undying love, comparing the beloved to a freshly bloomed rose and pledging devotion until the seas run dry. Burns adapted it from traditional verses he collected, blending folk tradition with personal feeling to create one of the most enduring romantic poems in the English language.

title: The Good-Morrow
author: John Donne
lines: I wonder, by my troth, what thou and I
Did, till we loved? were we not weaned till then?
But sucked on country pleasures, childishly?
Or snorted we in the Seven Sleepers' den?
'Twas so; but this, all pleasures fancies be.
If ever any beauty I did see,
Which I desired, and got, 'twas but a dream of thee.
And now good-morrow to our waking souls,
Which watch not one another out of fear;
For love, all love of other sights controls,
And makes one little room an everywhere.
Let sea-discoverers to new worlds have gone,
Let maps to other, worlds on worlds have shown,
Let us possess one world, each hath one, and is one.
background: John Donne (1572–1631) was an English metaphysical poet renowned for his intellectual wit and deeply felt emotion. 'The Good-Morrow' opens his 'Songs and Sonnets' collection and celebrates the moment two lovers wake together, suggesting that their love creates a world unto itself. Donne's metaphysical style weaves philosophical ideas with passionate feeling, making him a unique and influential voice in the history of love poetry.

title: When You Are Old
author: W. B. Yeats
lines: When you are old and grey and full of sleep,
And nodding by the fire, take down this book,
And slowly read, and dream of the soft look
Your eyes had once, and of their shadows deep;
How many loved your moments of glad grace,
And loved your beauty with love false or true,
But one man loved the pilgrim soul in you,
And loved the sorrows of your changing face;
And bending down beside the glowing bars,
Murmur, a little sadly, how Love fled
And paced upon the mountains overhead
And hid his face amid a crowd of stars.
background: William Butler Yeats (1865–1939) was an Irish poet and one of the foremost figures of 20th-century literature. This poem, written in 1891, was addressed to Maud Gonne, the Irish revolutionary whom Yeats loved deeply but who repeatedly refused his marriage proposals. It imagines her in old age, reflecting on those who loved her — and singling out Yeats as the one who loved her truest, including her imperfections. Quietly heartbreaking.

title: Sonnet 116
author: William Shakespeare
lines: Let me not to the marriage of true minds
Admit impediments. Love is not love
Which alters when it alteration finds,
Or bends with the remover to remove:
O no! it is an ever-fixed mark
That looks on tempests and is never shaken;
It is the star to every wand'ring bark,
Whose worth's unknown, although his height be taken.
Love's not Time's fool, though rosy lips and cheeks
Within his bending sickle's compass come:
Love alters not with his brief hours and weeks,
But bears it out even to the edge of doom.
If this be error and upon me proved,
I never writ, nor no man ever loved.
background: Shakespeare's Sonnet 116 is one of his most celebrated, written in the 1590s. It defines true love as something immovable — not swayed by change, time, or hardship. Often read at weddings, it argues that love which fades when tested was never real love at all. Shakespeare uses the metaphor of a guiding star for sailors to describe love as a fixed, dependable force in an uncertain world.

title: Love's Philosophy
author: Percy Bysshe Shelley
lines: The fountains mingle with the river
And the rivers with the ocean,
The winds of heaven mix for ever
With a sweet emotion;
Nothing in the world is single;
All things by a law divine
In one spirit meet and mingle.
Why not I with thine?
See the mountains kiss high heaven
And the waves clasp one another;
No sister-flower would be forgiven
If it disdained its brother;
And the sunlight clasps the earth
And the moonbeams kiss the sea:
What is all this sweet work worth
If thou kiss not me?
background: Percy Bysshe Shelley (1792–1822) was a major English Romantic poet. This playful yet earnest poem, written in 1819, uses the natural world — rivers meeting the sea, mountains kissing heaven — as an argument for why two lovers should unite. It was likely written during his relationship with Mary Shelley, author of Frankenstein. The poem's light touch and witty logic make it one of the most charming romantic lyrics of the Romantic era.

title: Wild Nights – Wild Nights!
author: Emily Dickinson
lines: Wild Nights – Wild Nights!
Were I with thee
Wild Nights should be
Our luxury!
Futile – the winds –
To a Heart in port –
Done with the Compass –
Done with the Chart!
Rowing in Eden –
Ah, the Sea!
Might I but moor – Tonight –
In Thee!
background: Emily Dickinson (1830–1886) was an American poet who lived in near-total seclusion, yet produced nearly 1,800 poems of extraordinary power. 'Wild Nights' is one of her most boldly passionate poems, using the metaphor of a ship finding safe harbour to express intense desire and the longing for union with a beloved. The poem was thought too daring to publish during her lifetime. Its identity of the recipient remains one of literature's great mysteries.

title: Annabel Lee
author: Edgar Allan Poe
lines: It was many and many a year ago,
In a kingdom by the sea,
That a maiden there lived whom you may know
By the name of Annabel Lee;
And this maiden she lived with no other thought
Than to love and be loved by me.
I was a child and she was a child,
In this kingdom by the sea,
But we loved with a love that was more than love —
I and my Annabel Lee —
With a love that the wingèd seraphs of Heaven
Coveted her and me.
And this was the reason that, long ago,
In this kingdom by the sea,
A wind blew out of a cloud, chilling
My beautiful Annabel Lee.
background: Edgar Allan Poe (1809–1849) was an American writer famous for his gothic tales and poetry. 'Annabel Lee' was the last complete poem he wrote before his death and is believed to be an elegy for his young wife Virginia Clemm, who died of tuberculosis in 1847. Despite its grief, the poem is a testament to love's power over death itself — Poe insists their bond transcends even the grave, making it one of literature's most haunting love poems.

title: Bright Star
author: John Keats
lines: Bright star, would I were stedfast as thou art—
Not in lone splendour hung aloft the night
And watching, with eternal lids apart,
Like nature's patient, sleepless Eremite,
The moving waters at their priestlike task
Of pure ablution round earth's human shores,
Or gazing on the new soft-fallen mask
Of snow upon the mountains and the moors—
No—yet still stedfast, still unchangeable,
Pillowed upon my fair love's ripening breast,
To feel for ever its soft fall and swell,
Awake for ever in a sweet unrest,
Still, still to hear her tender-taken breath,
And so live ever—or else swoon to death.
background: John Keats (1795–1821) was a key figure of English Romanticism, though he died tragically of tuberculosis aged just 25. 'Bright Star' is believed to have been written for Fanny Brawne, the woman he was deeply in love with but could never fully be with due to his illness. He reportedly wrote it on a blank page of Shakespeare's works. The poem longs for the constancy of a star, but wishes for that permanence while lying with his beloved — a heartbreakingly tender wish.

title: To My Dear and Loving Husband
author: Anne Bradstreet
lines: If ever two were one, then surely we.
If ever man were loved by wife, then thee.
If ever wife was happy in a man,
Compare with me, ye women, if you can.
I prize thy love more than whole mines of gold,
Or all the riches that the East doth hold.
My love is such that rivers cannot quench,
Nor ought but love from thee give recompense.
Thy love is such I can no way repay;
The heavens reward thee manifold, I pray.
Then while we live, in love let's so persevere,
That when we live no more, we may live ever.
background: Anne Bradstreet (1612–1672) was the first published poet in colonial America. This poem, written for her husband Simon Bradstreet, is one of the earliest and most heartfelt declarations of marital love in American literature. Bradstreet defied the Puritan expectation that women remain silent, instead writing openly about the joy and depth of her love. The poem's directness and warmth make it feel remarkably modern despite being over 350 years old.

title: The Sun Rising
author: John Donne
lines: Busy old fool, unruly sun,
Why dost thou thus,
Through windows, and through curtains call on us?
Must to thy motions lovers' seasons run?
Saucy pedantic wretch, go chide
Late school boys and sour prentices,
Go tell court-huntsmen that the king will ride,
Call country ants to harvest offices,
Love, all alike, no season knows nor clime,
Nor hours, days, months, which are the rags of time.
Thou, sun, art half as happy as we,
In that the world's contracted thus;
Thine age asks ease, and since thy duties be
To warm the world, that's done in warming us.
Shine here to us, and thou art everywhere;
This bed thy centre is, these walls thy sphere.
background: John Donne (1572–1631) wrote this delightful poem as a complaint against the sunrise interrupting two lovers in bed. With characteristic metaphysical wit, Donne argues that their love is so complete that it contains the entire world — the sun need only warm them to have done its job. It is one of the most joyful and confident love poems ever written, celebrating intimacy with a boldness unusual for its era.

title: Ode to My Socks... (excerpt — adapted)
author: Pablo Neruda
lines: I want to do with you
what spring does with the cherry trees.
Tonight I can write the most beautiful lines,
lines of love, clear as water.
I want you to know one thing:
you know how this is:
if I look at the crystal moon,
at the red branch of the slow autumn at my window,
if I touch near the fire the impalpable ash
or the wrinkled body of the log,
everything carries me to you,
as if everything that exists:
aromas, light, metals,
were little boats that sail
toward those isles of yours that wait for me.
background: Pablo Neruda (1904–1973) was a Nobel Prize-winning Chilean poet whose love poetry is celebrated worldwide for its rich, sensory language. This passage weaves together lines from several of his most beloved works, including verses from his 'One Hundred Love Sonnets'. Neruda had a gift for finding romance in the everyday — in objects, nature and small moments — turning the ordinary into something aching and beautiful.

title: Meeting at Night
author: Robert Browning
lines: The grey sea and the long black land;
And the yellow half-moon large and low;
And the startled little waves that leap
In fiery ringlets from their sleep,
As I gain the cove with pushing prow,
And quench its speed i' the slushy sand.
Then a mile of warm sea-scented beach;
Three fields to cross till a farm appears;
A tap at the pane, the quick sharp scratch
And blue spurt of a lighted match,
And a voice less loud, through its joys and fears,
Than the two hearts beating each to each!
background: Robert Browning (1812–1889) was a Victorian poet famous for his dramatic monologues. 'Meeting at Night' is a beautifully cinematic poem — it traces a lover's secret night-time journey across sea and fields to reach his beloved. The poem builds tension through vivid sensory detail, culminating in the quiet, electric moment of reunion. Browning was himself a devoted romantic, famously eloping with Elizabeth Barrett Browning after a passionate courtship conducted largely by letter.

title: Because I Could Not Stop for Death (excerpt)
author: Emily Dickinson
lines: Because I could not stop for Death –
He kindly stopped for me –
The Carriage held but just Ourselves –
And Immortality.
We slowly drove – He knew no haste
And I had put away
My labor and my leisure too,
For His Civility –
We passed the School, where Children strove
At Recess – in the Ring –
We passed the Fields of Gazing Grain –
We passed the Setting Sun –
background: Emily Dickinson (1830–1886) wrote this poem around 1863, personifying Death as a courteous gentleman taking her for a carriage ride into eternity. While not a conventional love poem, it speaks to the eternal journey two souls take together and the quiet dignity of devotion that outlasts life itself. It is widely considered one of the greatest poems in the American literary canon and a meditation on what truly endures.

title: Sonnet 29
author: William Shakespeare
lines: When, in disgrace with fortune and men's eyes,
I all alone beweep my outcast state,
And trouble deaf heaven with my bootless cries,
And look upon myself and curse my fate,
Wishing me like to one more rich in hope,
Featured like him, like him with friends possessed,
Desiring this man's art and that man's scope,
With what I most enjoy contented least;
Yet in these thoughts myself almost despising,
Haply I think on thee, and then my state,
Like to the lark at break of day arising
From sullen earth, sings hymns at heaven's gate;
For thy sweet love remembered such wealth brings
That then I scorn to change my state with kings.
background: Shakespeare's Sonnet 29 is a poem about the transformative power of love. The speaker begins in despair — feeling like an outcast, envying others and cursing his fate — yet the mere thought of his beloved lifts him instantly from misery to joy. It is one of Shakespeare's most relatable and emotionally direct sonnets, and a reminder that love can be the greatest comfort in times of self-doubt and hardship.

title: La Belle Dame sans Merci
author: John Keats
lines: O what can ail thee, knight-at-arms,
Alone and palely loitering?
The sedge has withered from the lake,
And no birds sing.
I met a lady in the meads,
Full beautiful — a faery's child,
Her hair was long, her foot was light,
And her eyes were wild.
I made a garland for her head,
And bracelets too, and fragrant zone;
She looked at me as she did love,
And made sweet moan.
And there she lulled me asleep,
And there I dreamed — Ah! woe betide! —
The latest dream I ever dreamt
On the cold hill side.
background: John Keats wrote this haunting ballad in 1819. It tells of a knight entranced and ultimately abandoned by a mysterious, beautiful woman — a 'beautiful lady without mercy.' The title is borrowed from a medieval French poem. Scholars believe it reflects Keats's own consuming love for Fanny Brawne and his fear that love, however beautiful, brings suffering. It is one of the most atmospheric and emotionally complex of all Romantic love poems.

title: To His Coy Mistress
author: Andrew Marvell
lines: Had we but world enough, and time,
This coyness, lady, were no crime.
We would sit down and think which way
To walk, and pass our long love's day.
Thou by the Indian Ganges' side
Shouldst rubies find; I by the tide
Of Humber would complain. I would
Love you ten years before the flood,
And you should, if you please, refuse
Till the conversion of the Jews.
My vegetable love should grow
Vaster than empires, and more slow;
But at my back I always hear
Time's wingèd chariot hurrying near.
background: Andrew Marvell (1621–1678) was an English metaphysical poet. 'To His Coy Mistress' is his most celebrated poem — a witty, sophisticated argument urging a reluctant lover to seize the day. Marvell argues that if they had eternity, he would gladly wait, but since time is short, they should love urgently and fully. It is both playful and philosophically rich, blending humour with genuine passion in a way that feels surprisingly modern.

title: Remember
author: Christina Rossetti
lines: Remember me when I am gone away,
Gone far away into the silent land;
When you can no more hold me by the hand,
Nor I half turn to go yet turning stay.
Remember me when no more day by day
You tell me of our future that you planned:
Only remember me; you understand
It will be late to counsel then or pray.
Yet if you should forget me for a while
And afterwards remember, do not grieve:
For if the darkness and corruption leave
A vestige of the thoughts that once I had,
Better by far you should forget and smile
Than that you should remember and be sad.
background: Christina Rossetti (1830–1894) was a Victorian poet known for her spiritual depth and lyrical beauty. 'Remember' is a tender farewell poem — the speaker asks to be remembered after death, but then, movingly, releases the loved one from that burden if remembering brings only grief. Rossetti was deeply religious and often wrote about love, death, and longing. This sonnet, with its selfless final turn, remains one of the most beloved Victorian poems.

title: somewhere i have never travelled
author: E. E. Cummings
lines: somewhere i have never travelled, gladly beyond
any experience, your eyes have their silence:
in your most frail gesture are things which enclose me,
or which i cannot touch because they are too near
your slightest look easily will unclose me
though i have closed myself as fingers,
you open always petal by petal myself as Spring opens
(touching skilfully, mysteriously) her first rose
or if your wish be to close me, i and
my life will shut very beautifully, suddenly,
as when the heart of this flower imagines
the snow carefully everywhere descending;
nothing which we are to perceive in this world equals
the power of your intense fragility
background: E. E. Cummings (1894–1962) broke every convention of punctuation and form to express emotion with raw immediacy. This poem captures the vulnerability of being deeply known by another person — how a beloved's smallest gesture can undo us. Cummings uses the metaphor of a flower opening to convey the paradox of love: that the person who has the power to unmake us is also the one who makes us most alive. It is quietly one of his most profound love poems.

title: A Valediction: Forbidding Mourning
author: John Donne
lines: As virtuous men pass mildly away,
And whisper to their souls to go,
Whilst some of their sad friends do say
The breath goes now, and some say, no:
So let us melt, and make no noise,
No tear-floods, nor sigh-tempests move,
'Twere profanation of our joys
To tell the laity our love.
Our two souls therefore, which are one,
Though I must go, endure not yet
A breach, but an expansion,
Like gold to airy thinness beat.
background: Written by John Donne (1572–1631) as a farewell poem to his wife Anne before a journey to France in 1611. Donne argues that their love is so deep and spiritual that physical separation cannot truly divide them — their souls are like a compass, always connected. He asks her not to grieve. It is considered one of the finest metaphysical poems ever written and a profound statement on the nature of true love between two people.

title: Sonnet 75
author: Edmund Spenser
lines: One day I wrote her name upon the strand,
But came the waves and washèd it away:
Again I wrote it with a second hand,
But came the tide, and made my pains his prey.
'Vain man,' said she, 'that dost in vain assay,
A mortal thing so to immortalize,
For I myself shall like to this decay,
And eek my name be wipèd out likewise.'
'Not so,' quod I, 'let baser things devise
To die in dust, but you shall live by fame:
My verse your virtues rare shall eternize,
And in the heavens write your glorious name.'
background: Edmund Spenser (1552–1599) was an Elizabethan poet best known for 'The Faerie Queene.' This sonnet is from his 'Amoretti' sequence, written during his courtship of Elizabeth Boyle, whom he later married. In it, the lover writes his beloved's name in sand, only for the sea to erase it — but he promises that his poetry will immortalise her forever. It echoes Shakespeare's Sonnet 18 and is one of the most elegant love pledges in Renaissance literature.

title: My True Love Hath My Heart
author: Sir Philip Sidney
lines: My true love hath my heart, and I have his,
By just exchange one for another given:
I hold his dear, and mine he cannot miss,
There never was a better bargain driven:
My true love hath my heart, and I have his.
His heart in me keeps him and me in one,
My heart in him his thoughts and senses guides:
He loves my heart, for once it was his own,
I cherish his because in me it bides:
My true love hath my heart, and I have his.
background: Sir Philip Sidney (1554–1586) was an Elizabethan poet, soldier, and courtier — considered the ideal Renaissance gentleman. This short lyric from his prose romance 'The Arcadia' celebrates the perfect mutuality of love: two hearts exchanged so completely that each belongs to the other. Its gentle simplicity and circular structure — beginning and ending with the same line — give it a feeling of completeness that mirrors its subject. It remains one of the most charming Renaissance love lyrics.

title: Astrophil and Stella, Sonnet 1
author: Sir Philip Sidney
lines: Loving in truth, and fain in verse my love to show,
That she dear She might take some pleasure of my pain,
Pleasure might cause her read, reading might make her know,
Knowledge might pity win, and pity grace obtain,
I sought fit words to paint the blackest face of woe,
Studying inventions fine, her wits to entertain,
Oft turning others' leaves, to see if thence would flow
Some fresh and fruitful showers upon my sun-burn'd brain.
But words came halting forth, wanting Invention's stay,
Invention, Nature's child, fled step-dame Study's blows,
And others' feet still seem'd but strangers in my way.
Thus, great with child to speak, and helpless in my throes,
Biting my truant pen, beating myself for spite,
'Fool,' said my Muse to me, 'look in thy heart and write.'
background: Sir Philip Sidney's 'Astrophil and Stella' (1591) is one of the first great English sonnet sequences, written for Penelope Devereux, whom he loved but could not marry. The opening sonnet is a meta-poem about the struggle to write love poetry, ending with the Muse's famous advice: 'look in thy heart and write.' It established the English sonnet tradition and showed that authentic feeling, not borrowed phrases, is the true source of great love poetry.

title: Sonnet 130
author: William Shakespeare
lines: My mistress' eyes are nothing like the sun;
Coral is far more red than her lips' red;
If snow be white, why then her breasts are dun;
If hairs be wires, black wires grow on her head.
I have seen roses damasked, red and white,
But no such roses see I in her cheeks;
And in some perfumes is there more delight
Than in the breath that from my mistress reeks.
I love to hear her speak, yet well I know
That music hath a far more pleasing sound;
I grant I never saw a goddess go;
My mistress, when she walks, treads on the ground:
And yet, by heaven, I think my love as rare
As any she belied with false compare.
background: Shakespeare's Sonnet 130 is a witty anti-love poem that subverts the clichés of the day. Rather than comparing his beloved to the sun or roses, Shakespeare cheerfully admits she has none of these qualities — yet his love is no less real or rare for it. It is a celebration of loving someone for who they are, not an idealised image. In an age of exaggerated flattery, it remains a refreshingly honest and charming declaration of real love.

title: The Passionate Shepherd to His Love
author: Christopher Marlowe
lines: Come live with me and be my love,
And we will all the pleasures prove,
That valleys, groves, hills, and fields,
Woods or steepy mountain yields.
And we will sit upon the rocks,
Seeing the shepherds feed their flocks,
By shallow rivers to whose falls
Melodious birds sing madrigals.
And I will make thee beds of roses
And a thousand fragrant posies,
A cap of flowers, and a kirtle
Embroidered all with leaves of myrtle.
background: Christopher Marlowe (1564–1593) was Shakespeare's great contemporary and rival, a brilliant playwright and poet who died aged 29. This pastoral lyric, probably written in the late 1580s, is one of the most famous invitations in English literature — a shepherd promising his beloved a paradise of nature's pleasures if she will only come and live with him. Its joyful, carefree spirit has made it enduringly popular, and it inspired several famous poetic replies, including one by Sir Walter Raleigh.

title: Love After Love
author: Derek Walcott
lines: The time will come
when, with elation,
you will greet yourself arriving
at your own door, in your own mirror,
and each will smile at the other's welcome,
and say, sit here. Eat.
You will love again the stranger who was yourself.
Give wine. Give bread. Give back your heart
to itself, to the stranger who has loved you
all your life, whom you ignored
for another, who knows you by heart.
Take down the love letters from the bookshelf,
the photographs, the desperate notes,
peel your own image from the mirror.
Sit. Feast on your life.
background: Derek Walcott (1930–2017) was a Saint Lucian poet and Nobel Prize laureate. 'Love After Love' is perhaps his most beloved poem — a gentle instruction to rediscover oneself after a relationship ends, to welcome back the self that was neglected in the giving of love to another. It is a poem about self-restoration and the quiet love that remains when romantic love has passed. Its warmth and wisdom give Amourly a note of emotional depth and maturity.
